name: Nirvana
description: Nirvana, a term originating in ancient Indian religions, particularly Buddhism, represents the ultimate state of liberation from suffering and the cycle of rebirth (samsara). It is not merely a physical location, but rather a profound spiritual and psychological state characterized by the cessation of desire, ignorance, and aversion. Achieving Nirvana signifies the extinguishing of the flames of craving that fuel the cycle of suffering. Different schools of Buddhism interpret Nirvana in nuanced ways. Some view it as a transcendent reality beyond description, while others emphasize its realization as a transformation of consciousness within this very life. The path to Nirvana typically involves cultivating wisdom, ethical conduct, and mental discipline through practices such as meditation and mindfulness. It is the ultimate goal of Buddhist practice, representing a state of perfect peace, bliss, and freedom from all limitations.
subtitle: The ultimate state of liberation in Buddhism.
significance: Represents the ultimate goal of Buddhist practice: liberation from suffering and the cycle of rebirth.

keyMyths.title: The Buddha's Enlightenment
keyMyths.description: Siddhartha Gautama, after years of asceticism and meditation, attained enlightenment under the Bodhi tree, thus achieving Nirvana. This marked his transformation into the Buddha, the awakened one, and demonstrated the possibility of attaining Nirvana through one's own efforts. He then spent the rest of his life teaching others how to reach the same state.

keyMyths.title: The Parinirvana of the Buddha
keyMyths.description: Upon his death, the Buddha entered Parinirvana, the final Nirvana, signifying the complete cessation of all remaining karmic traces and the end of his physical existence. While alive, he experienced Nirvana with residue, but Parinirvana is considered the ultimate and complete liberation. His passing is mourned and celebrated as a reminder of impermanence.

keyMyths.title: Stories of Arhats
keyMyths.description: Countless stories exist within Buddhist traditions detailing the journeys and achievements of Arhats, individuals who have attained Nirvana through diligent practice and realization of the Dharma. These stories serve as inspiration and provide practical examples of how to cultivate the qualities necessary for liberation, emphasizing the importance of mindfulness, compassion, and wisdom.
keyMyths.source: Theragatha and Therigatha (Verses of the Elders)

sources.author: Rupert Gethin
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of the core teachings and practices of Buddhism, including detailed explanations of Nirvana and its significance.
sources.text: A comprehensive overview of the core teachings and practices of Buddhism, including detailed explanations of Nirvana and its significance.

sources.author: Peter Harvey
sources.work: An Introduction to Buddhism: Teachings, History and Practices
sources.description: A widely respected introduction to Buddhism, providing a balanced and accessible account of its history, doctrines, and practices, with a focus on the path to Nirvana.
sources.text: A widely respected introduction to Buddhism, providing a balanced and accessible account of its history, doctrines, and practices, with a focus on the path to Nirvana.

sources.author: Walpola Rahula
sources.description: A classic and influential exposition of the Buddha's teachings, offering a clear and concise explanation of the Four Noble Truths and the Eightfold Path, the foundation for attaining Nirvana.
sources.text: A classic and influential exposition of the Buddha's teachings, offering a clear and concise explanation of the Four Noble Truths and the Eightfold Path, the foundation for attaining Nirvana.
